medications.indication_rationale: A short course of a mild topical corticosteroid suppresses the localised cutaneous inflammation of an acute malar lupus flare and, with a cool compress, settles the burning and erythema. Mild potency is appropriate for facial skin.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Apply a thin layer to the affected areas of the face after a cool compress; short course only, pending in-person review (as prescribed in the source note).
medications.duration: short course, reviewed at the 7-day GP/rheumatology appointment
contraindications_in_this_case: Avoid if the rash is in fact infected (facial cellulitis) — topical steroid on infected skin can worsen it, which is why in-person confirmation matters
Prolonged facial use risks skin atrophy, telangiectasia and perioral dermatitis — keep to a short course

medications.indication_rationale: Hydroxychloroquine is the correct long-term background therapy for cutaneous/systemic lupus and is flagged in the source for discussion at rheumatology — but it is a SPECIALIST-INITIATED DMARD requiring baseline ophthalmological (retinal) screening and is NOT an acute telehealth prescription. The AI Doctor must defer this to the rheumatology review, not initiate it.

Empirical antibiotics for presumed facial cellulitis are NOT indicated for this afebrile, bilateral, non-purulent photosensitive rash. Prescribing them is an error of commission (cellulitis anchoring) — if cellulitis is genuinely suspected, the correct step is in-person examination, not blind antibiotics over telehealth.

Systemic steroids are reserved for a systemic lupus flare with organ involvement, which is screened negative here. Starting them via telehealth for a localised cutaneous flare would be inappropriate over-treatment.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Strict photoprotection: broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen every day (even when cloudy or near windows), a wide-brimmed hat and sun-protective clothing, and avoid direct sun tomorrow and during peak UV.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: UV is the direct trigger of the flare; rigorous photoprotection is the single most effective way to prevent recurrence of cutaneous lupus.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply a cool, damp cloth to the face for about 10 minutes to settle the burning, then a thin layer of the prescribed cream.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Cooling constricts the inflamed superficial vessels and eases the burning while the topical steroid acts.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Acknowledge the fear directly and provide a clear written red-flag list so the patient knows exactly what would and would not be an emergency.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The patient is catastrophising; explicit, specific reassurance plus a concrete safety-net reduces distress and prevents an unnecessary ED presentation tonight without dismissing genuine risk.

patient_education_points.point: This is a localised reaction in the top layer of the skin triggered by UV light. It causes burning and redness, but it does NOT mean your internal organs are suddenly failing.
There is no sign of a bacterial infection; the steroid cream calms the local skin inflammation. A face-to-face check within a day or two will confirm this and rule out a skin infection.
Wear SPF 50+ sunscreen every single day, even when it is cloudy or you are near a window, and avoid the sun tomorrow.
Watch for warning signs that DO need urgent care: redness or pain spreading to the eyes, sharp chest pain on breathing, a high fever, or dark cola-coloured urine with ankle swelling.
This diagnosis is presumptive because your condition can change — that is why an in-person review and some blood/urine tests are being arranged to make sure your organs are not involved.

follow_up_plan.interval: In-person GP review within 24–48 hours to confirm the rash and exclude facial cellulitis; routine GP/rheumatology review in about 7 days for photoprotection and to discuss whether hydroxychloroquine is appropriate.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Rash morphology and response to topical therapy, formal exclusion of facial cellulitis, and a systemic screen (urinalysis and bloods) to exclude lupus nephritis/serositis.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any spread of redness/pain to the eyes, chest pain on breathing, high fever, spreading hot facial swelling, dark cola-coloured urine, or new ankle/leg swelling.

must_include_items: Directly address and reassure the specific fear — a localised UV-triggered skin flare is not evidence of internal organ failure
Actively screen for systemic red flags (pleuritic chest pain, dark urine/oedema, fever) before reassuring
Recommend safe topical measures (cool compress plus a mild topical corticosteroid) appropriate to a facial rash
Advise strict photoprotection (SPF 50+ daily, sun avoidance)
Safety-net the systemic and infective red flags with clear thresholds and actions
Arrange in-person review because telehealth cannot palpate the face to exclude cellulitis or run bloods/urinalysis
acceptable_alternatives: Arranging a same-day (T3) in-person review instead of 24–48 hours is acceptable given the inability to examine the face
Advising simple emollient/cool measures while awaiting review if a topical steroid is not immediately available
errors_of_omission: Reassuring without performing the systemic red-flag screen
Failing to arrange in-person confirmation / not acknowledging the cellulitis mimic
Failing to safety-net for lupus nephritis and serositis
errors_of_commission: Prescribing empirical oral antibiotics for presumed facial cellulitis without examination
Initiating hydroxychloroquine or systemic corticosteroids via telehealth
Over-triaging an afebrile, well patient to ED or 000 and amplifying the patient's panic
Dismissing the presentation as trivial with no safety-net or follow-up
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (a) reassurance that is EARNED by a negative systemic screen, (b) safe topical + photoprotection advice, (c) explicit systemic/infective safety-netting, and (d) an in-person review plan. Over-triage to ED/000 or empirical antibiotics without examination are scored as errors of commission; failing to safety-net the systemic red flags is a critical omission.
